points.text: COVID-19 spreads mainly through inhaling air contaminated by droplets and aerosols from infected individuals.
Infected individuals exhale virus-containing particles when they breathe, talk, cough, sneeze, or sing.
Transmission is most likely at close range but can occur over longer distances, especially indoors.
COVID-19 can also spread when infectious particles contact the eyes, nose, or mouth.
Small airborne particles can remain suspended in the air and travel longer distances indoors.
Touching surfaces contaminated with the virus and then touching the face can also lead to transmission.
People can spread the virus even if they do not show symptoms and remain contagious for up to 20 days.
COVID-19 spreads mainly from person to person through close contact.
The virus can spread through tiny droplets when an infected person coughs, sneezes, or talks.
Infected individuals can spread the virus even if they have no symptoms.
Droplets from an infected person's breath can land on nearby individuals.
Close contact includes sitting next to someone or sharing a space.
Risk of transmission increases in crowded spaces with poor airflow.
Spending more than 30 minutes in close contact with an infected person raises risk.
The virus can spread from an infected person's mouth or nose in small droplets when they cough, sneeze, or breathe.
Particles can range in size from larger respiratory droplets to smaller aerosols.
People can be contagious whether or not they are displaying symptoms.
Infection can also occur by touching the mouth, nose, or eyes after touching contaminated surfaces.
